title: How spells work
subtitle: The folk-magic shape used in this library: real objects, plain words, a small ceremony.
excerpt: A reference article for the spell practices in the library. Covers what a spell is in this context, what it isn't, the four-part shape (intention, object, words, closing), and the materials you'll see across the practices. Read once; the practice scripts assume it.
sourceNotes: Folk magic and candle work are public-domain practices with many cultural lineages — European hedge-witch traditions, Appalachian folk magic, hoodoo, Hellenistic candle work, modern eclectic witchcraft. The shape used in this library follows the general candle-and-intention pattern documented across these traditions. Specific named traditions are credited per practice where adapted directly.

practice.whenToUse: Read once before using any spell practice in the library. Re-read if the candle work or the closing instructions feel unfamiliar.

content.text: A spell in this library is a small folk ceremony: real objects, plain spoken words, a clear beginning and end. A candle gets lit. A sentence gets said. Salt sits in a bowl, or a slip of paper gets folded into a pocket. Ten minutes start to finish, usually less.

This reading covers what a spell is in this context, what it isn't, the four-part shape, and the materials you'll see across the practices. The individual spell scripts assume it. They open with the intention, name the materials, and get on with the ceremony.
What a spell is here

A spell in this library is a focusing ritual. The objects are real: a candle, a bowl of salt, a glass of water, a folded paper. The words are plain: "I let this go. I bring this in." The point is to give the body and the mind something to do while the intention lands.

What it isn't: occult performance, theatrical incantation, claimed psychic effect on other people. The library uses spell work the way it uses journal practice: a private, daily-life-sized act with a clear purpose. The ceremony is the point. The result over time is the practice.
The four-part shape
Every spell in this library has the same shape. Each script names the specific version it uses, but the underlying four parts run across all of them.
1. Set up

Gather the materials. Set them in front of you. Take three slow breaths. Settle the room — phone away, door closed, lamp on if it helps. The set-up names the spell as a small bounded event with a beginning and an end.
2. Name the intention

Say or write the specific intention. One short sentence. "I release the fear that the night is dangerous." "I welcome enough money to pay the bills without tightness." Not a list, not a wish-tower. One sentence, plain.
3. Do the ceremony

Light the candle. Pour the salt. Fold the paper into your pocket. Each spell has its own physical action — the action is the body's part of the work, the same way tapping is the body's part of an EFT round. Move through the action slowly. The pace is part of the ceremony.
4. Close

Every spell has a closing — blow out the candle, wash the salt down the sink, bury or burn the paper, put the object where you'll see it. The close marks the ceremony as finished. Don't skip it. The end is as important as the beginning.
The materials you'll see
Candle.
Any unscented household candle. A tea light is fine. The flame holds attention; blowing it out ends the spell.
Salt.
Any clean cooking salt. Used for protection or cleansing — a small bowl on the bedside table, a pinch in each corner of a room, dissolved in water and poured away.
Water.
A glass or bowl of tap water. Used for receiving — sit it by the bed or on a window sill. Washed away at the end of the practice.
Paper and pen.

For written intentions. The act of handwriting is part of the practice — type a note on a phone and the body doesn't engage the same way. Disposed of at the end of the spell, kept in a pocket, or filed in a notebook.
Safety with flame

Candles get hot. Use a heat-proof saucer. Don't leave a lit candle unattended; never leave it overnight. If a spell takes longer than the candle, blow the candle out and relight the next day. A tea light in a holder is the lowest-risk option.
Where this comes from

Folk magic and candle work have many cultural lineages — European hedge-witch traditions, Appalachian folk magic, hoodoo, Hellenistic candle work, modern eclectic witchcraft. The shape used here follows the general candle-and-intention pattern documented across these traditions. Where a specific spell in this library is drawn from a named tradition, that's credited in the spell's own provenance section. The general lineage is the practical inheritance of women keeping small ceremonies in kitchens, gardens, and bedrooms for as long as anyone has been keeping records.
